questions.q: What is the main purpose of a bill of exchange?
opts: (A) To borrow money from the bank
(B) To transfer money from one person to another
(C) To buy goods on credit
(D) To sell goods on credit
questions.explanation: A bill of exchange is a written order by one person to another to pay a certain sum of money at a specified date or on demand. It is used to transfer money from one person to another.

questions.q: Who are the parties involved in a bill of exchange?
opts: (A) Drawer, Drawee, and Payee
(B) Drawer, Drawee, and Endorser
(C) Drawer, Drawee, and Holder
(D) Drawer, Drawee, and Endorsee
questions.explanation: The parties involved in a bill of exchange are the drawer, drawee, and payee. The drawer is the person who draws the bill, the drawee is the person who is obligated to pay the bill, and the payee is the person who receives the payment.
questions.topic: Parties to a Bill of Exchange

questions.q: What is the difference between a clean bill of exchange and a conditional bill of exchange?
opts: (A) A clean bill is paid on demand, while a conditional bill is paid at a specified date
(B) A clean bill is paid at a specified date, while a conditional bill is paid on demand
(C) A clean bill is used for domestic transactions, while a conditional bill is used for international transactions
(D) A clean bill is used for international transactions, while a conditional bill is used for domestic transactions
questions.explanation: A clean bill of exchange is paid on demand, while a conditional bill of exchange is paid at a specified date. A clean bill is used for domestic transactions, while a conditional bill is used for international transactions.

questions.q: What is the process of endorsing a bill of exchange?
opts: (A) Signing the bill in the presence of the drawee
(B) Signing the bill in the presence of the holder
(C) Signing the bill on the back of the bill
(D) Signing the bill on the front of the bill
questions.explanation: Endorsing a bill of exchange involves signing the bill on the back of the bill. This is done to transfer the ownership of the bill to another person.

questions.q: What is the difference between a holder in due course and an ordinary holder?
opts: (A) A holder in due course has priority over an ordinary holder
(B) A holder in due course has no priority over an ordinary holder
(C) A holder in due course is paid on demand, while an ordinary holder is paid at a specified date
(D) A holder in due course is paid at a specified date, while an ordinary holder is paid on demand
questions.explanation: A holder in due course has priority over an ordinary holder. This means that if there are any disputes or issues with the payment of the bill, the holder in due course is protected and has priority over the ordinary holder.
questions.topic: Holder in Due Course vs Ordinary Holder

questions.q: What is the effect of a bill of exchange being dishonored?
opts: (A) The drawee is obligated to pay the bill
(B) The payee is obligated to pay the bill
(C) The drawer is obligated to pay the bill
(D) The bill is considered void and cannot be enforced
questions.explanation: If a bill of exchange is dishonored, it means that the drawee has failed to pay the bill. In this case, the bill is considered void and cannot be enforced.

questions.q: What is the difference between a bill of exchange and a promissory note?
opts: (A) A bill of exchange is used for domestic transactions, while a promissory note is used for international transactions
(B) A bill of exchange is used for international transactions, while a promissory note is used for domestic transactions
(C) A bill of exchange is used to transfer money from one person to another, while a promissory note is used to borrow money
(D) A bill of exchange is used to borrow money, while a promissory note is used to transfer money from one person to another
questions.explanation: A bill of exchange is used to transfer money from one person to another, while a promissory note is used to borrow money. A bill of exchange is typically used for international transactions, while a promissory note is used for domestic transactions.
questions.topic: Bill of Exchange vs Promissory Note

questions.q: What is the effect of a bill of exchange being protested?
opts: (A) The bill is considered void and cannot be enforced
(B) The drawee is obligated to pay the bill
(C) The payee is obligated to pay the bill
(D) The bill is considered null and void, and the drawer is not responsible for the payment
questions.explanation: If a bill of exchange is protested, it means that the drawee has failed to pay the bill and the bill has been declared null and void. In this case, the drawer is not responsible for the payment.

questions.q: What is the role of a notary public in the process of a bill of exchange?
opts: (A) To authenticate the bill
(B) To witness the endorsement
(C) To verify the identity of the parties involved
(D) To provide a guarantee for the payment
questions.explanation: A notary public plays a role in authenticating the bill of exchange. They verify the identity of the parties involved and ensure that the bill is properly executed and completed.
questions.topic: Role of Notary Public in Bills of Exchange

questions.q: What is the difference between a bill of exchange and an invoice?
opts: (A) A bill of exchange is used for domestic transactions, while an invoice is used for international transactions
(B) A bill of exchange is used to transfer money from one person to another, while an invoice is used to request payment
(C) A bill of exchange is used to borrow money, while an invoice is used to sell goods
(D) A bill of exchange is used to sell goods, while an invoice is used to borrow money
questions.explanation: A bill of exchange is used to transfer money from one person to another, while an invoice is used to request payment. A bill of exchange is typically used for international transactions, while an invoice is used for domestic transactions.

questions.q: What is the effect of a bill of exchange being lost or destroyed?
opts: (A) The bill is considered void and cannot be enforced
(B) The drawee is obligated to pay the bill
(C) The payee is obligated to pay the bill
(D) The bill can be reissued and the original bill is considered null and void
questions.explanation: If a bill of exchange is lost or destroyed, it can be reissued and the original bill is considered null and void. The reissued bill is considered a new bill and is enforceable, while the original bill is no longer valid.
questions.topic: Loss or Destruction of Bills of Exchange

questions.q: What is the difference between a bill of exchange and a letter of credit?
opts: (A) A bill of exchange is used for domestic transactions, while a letter of credit is used for international transactions
(B) A bill of exchange is used to transfer money from one person to another, while a letter of credit is used to request payment
(C) A bill of exchange is used to borrow money, while a letter of credit is used to sell goods
(D) A bill of exchange is used to sell goods, while a letter of credit is used to borrow money
questions.explanation: A bill of exchange is used to transfer money from one person to another, while a letter of credit is used to request payment. A bill of exchange is typically used for international transactions, while a letter of credit is used for domestic transactions.
questions.topic: Bill of Exchange vs Letter of Credit

questions.q: What is the effect of a bill of exchange being overdue?
opts: (A) The drawee is obligated to pay the bill
(B) The payee is obligated to pay the bill
(C) The bill is considered void and cannot be enforced
(D) The bill is considered null and void, and the drawer is not responsible for the payment
questions.explanation: If a bill of exchange is overdue, it means that the payment is delayed and the bill is considered void. In this case, the bill cannot be enforced and the drawer is not responsible for the payment.

questions.q: What is the role of a bank in the process of a bill of exchange?
opts: (A) To authenticate the bill
(B) To witness the endorsement
(C) To verify the identity of the parties involved
(D) To provide a guarantee for the payment
questions.explanation: A bank plays a role in providing a guarantee for the payment of a bill of exchange. They act as an intermediate party and ensure that the payment is made to the payee.
questions.topic: Role of Bank in Bills of Exchange

questions.q: What is the difference between a bill of exchange and a check?
opts: (A) A bill of exchange is used for domestic transactions, while a check is used for international transactions
(B) A bill of exchange is used to transfer money from one person to another, while a check is used to request payment
(C) A bill of exchange is used to borrow money, while a check is used to sell goods
(D) A bill of exchange is used to sell goods, while a check is used to borrow money
questions.explanation: A bill of exchange is used to transfer money from one person to another, while a check is used to request payment. A bill of exchange is typically used for international transactions, while a check is used for domestic transactions.

questions.q: What is the effect of a bill of exchange being compromised?
opts: (A) The bill is considered void and cannot be enforced
(B) The drawee is obligated to pay the bill
(C) The payee is obligated to pay the bill
(D) The bill is considered null and void, and the drawer is not responsible for the payment
questions.explanation: If a bill of exchange is compromised, it means that the payment is delayed or not made. In this case, the bill is considered null and void, and the drawer is not responsible for the payment.

questions.q: What is the difference between a bill of exchange and a promissory note with a bill of exchange clause?
opts: (A) A bill of exchange is used for domestic transactions, while a promissory note with a bill of exchange clause is used for international transactions
(B) A bill of exchange is used to transfer money from one person to another, while a promissory note with a bill of exchange clause is used to request payment
(C) A bill of exchange is used to borrow money, while a promissory note with a bill of exchange clause is used to sell goods
(D) A bill of exchange is used to sell goods, while a promissory note with a bill of exchange clause is used to borrow money
questions.explanation: A bill of exchange is used to transfer money from one person to another, while a promissory note with a bill of exchange clause is used to request payment. A bill of exchange is typically used for international transactions, while a promissory note with a bill of exchange clause is used for domestic transactions.
questions.topic: Bill of Exchange vs Promissory Note with Bill of Exchange Clause
